Chapter 7 The Bearing That Preferred Complaints
Bronn woke to the low, habitual complaint of a bearing somewhere along the inner curve—a sound that meant nothing catastrophic and therefore could linger for exactly three breaths, maybe four if the day felt generous. They used all three, flat on their back in the nest of quilts and scavenged padding slung against the Chamber’s inner skin, watching the dull procession of indicator lights roll past like a slow constellation that had long ago given up on urgency.
On the fourth breath the complaint lengthened, acquired a dry, almost conversational edge. That shifted it from “ignore” to “it will mutter at me later.” Bronn rolled out of the nest, bare feet meeting the scuffed, warm plating of the maintenance ring, and hooked one hand into a webbed strap while the artificial gravity pretended to consider its options this cycle. A screwdriver bumped their shoulder, bounced lazily, and was caught on the second try with the resigned patience of someone who had done this dance before.
They padded along the curve, passing the familiar markers of their own previous irritations: the ambiguous stain in the metal where a sealant had cured badly and then perfectly, the three mismatched bolts holding down a panel nobody else would ever look under, the hand-drawn arrow pointing to a junction box accompanied by, in their own cramped handwriting, “no.”
The sound resolved as they approached the eastern services bulge, where cooling and waste exchange traded slow, invisible pulses that felt almost like gossip. Hannibal’s voice drifted in through the conduit above, tinny from the duct speakers.
“Bronn, your friend is making a new noise.”
“My friends make many noises,” Bronn said, half to the air, half to the swelling panel where the bearing sat behind. “You’ll have to be more specific.”
“The third main,” Hannibal replied after a pause that meant they had turned to some status board and squinted figuratively at it. “The one you promoted from secondary without consulting the union.”
Bronn pressed their forehead to the panel. The vibration came through as a fine, toothy buzz, irregular but flirting with pattern, like something testing how much attention it could borrow. They could feel the spent heat moving under their skin, lingering longer than strictly necessary.
“I demoted the union,” Bronn said. “The bearing was always doing this. It’s just trying to get reassigned to something glamorous.”
Thunk appeared from the other direction, shoes squeaking faintly, hands full of nothing but potential interference. They moved carefully in the partial curve, still over-aware of where “down” currently thought it was. Rodzaki trailed behind, attention distributed across every surface with greedy interest that refused to rush.
“Is that it?” Thunk asked, stopping a little too close to Bronn’s elbow. “The—” They made an imprecise gesture that could have been “torus,” “panel,” or “entire project of existence arranged in a ring.”
“This,” Bronn said, patting the panel as if soothing a pet that had opinions, “is the part of the Chamber that complains when the rest of it forgets to be dramatic. Give me the small wrench.”
They did not look back. After a brief silence that felt like two people looking at one another and then at a pile of unfamiliar tools, Rodzaki said, “Which one is ‘small’ in this dialect?”
“The one that looks least confident,” Bronn said. “You’ll know it by its desire to be useful in situations beyond its design.”
Thunk rummaged through the magnetic strip of metal that held a holy nonsense of implements along the curve. Something clanged, stayed attached by its tether, was pulled down again. Eventually a wrench with chipped yellow paint and an apologetic angle to its handle was pressed into Bronn’s hand.
“Acceptable,” Bronn said. “Now hold onto something that cannot be replaced.”
Rodzaki eyed the nearest handhold as if it might disagree with that assessment, chose instead to plant their back against the inner rail, one hand on a conduit that hummed with stable, entirely disinterested power. Thunk grabbed the same rail Bronn’s foot rooted against, fingers whitening just enough to suggest negotiation.
Bronn popped the panel’s fasteners with practiced disrespect. The sheet swung out, revealing the layered mess within: pipes wrapped in insulation tape that had outlived its branding, conduits tagged in three different handwriting styles, a little cluster of sensors behaving like gossiping neighbors who had seen better days. The bearing in question sat half-embedded in a support brace, its casing discolored around one edge like a long-healed bruise that still enjoyed reminding everyone it existed.
“Is it supposed to be that color?” Thunk asked.
“It’s supposed to be this color,” Bronn said, tapping the least alarming portion. “It has evolved. You’ll find the same with people. Hold the light.”
Thunk fumbled for the shaft-mounted lamp, thumbed it on too bright, then dimmed it until the interior glowed with a patient yellow that felt almost forgiving. Bronn wedged the wrench onto a securing collar and leaned in, feeling the familiar resistance, the less familiar tremor that lingered like an unanswered question.
“This is your job?” Rodzaki asked, not unkindly. Their voice carried a shade of disbelief that had more to do with scale than with doubt.
“This is the part of my job that believes in direct cause and effect,” Bronn said. “The rest of it is more like talking to a plant that occasionally rearranges your furniture and refuses to explain why.”
They cracked the collar. The bearing gave up a thin, metallic whine and then a soft clunk as it loosened in its seat, as if relieved to finally admit something. A wash of warmer air rolled out, smelling faintly of recirculated metals and someone else’s breakfast that had taken its time departing.
“Does that mean something?” Thunk asked. “The smell.”
“It means you’re downwind of everything you thought vanished,” Bronn said. “Hand me the cloth. No, the less clean one. I’m not going to insult it.”
Hannibal’s voice came again, marginally more amused. “I see a fluctuation in your partial differential dignity.”
“That’s just me standing up too fast,” Bronn replied, wiggling the bearing free and cradling it in the cloth like something that might change its mind. “How’s your own nonsense.”
“Perfectly average,” Hannibal said. “Which is all we ask of systems handling high-risk transitions between ontological registers.”
Rodzaki blinked. “Is that a joke?”
“In their language it’s flirting,” Bronn said. “They’re proud of their averages. Keeps the Station from feeling neglected.”
“Sentinel Station is fine,” Hannibal said, with the tone of someone adjusting a tie that nobody could see. “It is present, attentive, and delighted by small variances. Rodzaki, Thunk—if you look up and ten degrees to your left, that’s one of its main observation spines.”
They both looked. Through a gap where the Chamber’s inner surface opened into a transparent bulge, the Station presented itself not as a monolith but as an arrangement of spines, dishes, and articulated frames floating around a more modest core. Cables and support struts unfurled like a metal forest, flexible enough that motion could be inferred but not seen, lingering just out of insistence.
“It’s…closer than I thought,” Thunk said. “I assumed it was more…distant.”
“It finds that an unproductive posture,” Hannibal said. “How is my favorite third main bearing.”
“Dry, entitled, seeking attention,” Bronn said. “We are adjusting its expectations.”
They set the bearing on a low shelf that had once been a crate and now had aspirations to be a workbench, perhaps on alternate days. Thunk leaned over it, then leaned back as if it might take offense at being stared at too directly.
“Do you replace it?” they asked. “Or negotiate.”
“Both,” Bronn said. They picked up a narrow brush and a small bottle of lubricant labeled in a language that no one present had learned on purpose. “Parts are like people. They last longer if they feel they have options.”
Rodzaki glanced around the Chamber as if seeing it anew: the toroidal walkway cluttered with tools and memory; the hanging plants coaxed along pipes in defiance of official airflow diagrams; the hammocked storage nets holding objects that were either spare parts or art pieces, depending on which hand grabbed them first and how urgently. Along the outer curve, panels folded away from the hull showed slices of machinery in progressive intimacy, some wide open, some barely cracked, all refusing to hurry.
“And you live here,” Rodzaki said, more a mapping statement than a question.
“Where else would I live,” Bronn replied mildly. “The Chamber gets anxious if I’m gone too long. Things start arranging themselves for efficiency. We can’t have that.”
Thunk laughed, short and genuine. “Is that bad.”
“It’s boring,” Bronn said. “Everything lined up by function. No cross-talk. No seat cushions made out of obsolete insulation. The Chamber is at its best when it has to wonder whether the pile of coils also wants to be a chair.”
Hannibal cleared their throat in a way that suggested a microphone being politely adjusted. “Speaking of efficiency, I am preparing a demonstration sequence on Sentinel Station for our guests. Bronn, are you done domestically intimidating the hardware.”
“Insofar as it accepts my authority,” Bronn said, sliding the bearing back into place with a little twist that felt like mutual concession. “There. It has been heard, indulged, and reminded of its job. You still buzz at me, I downgrade you to counterweight.”
They tightened the collar, wiped off the excess lubricant with the cloth that now carried a faint new warmth, then nodded once to the bearing like a shared secret had been acknowledged and agreed to remain unspoken. Thunk shut off the lamp; the interior shadows settled back into their accustomed densities, slightly rearranged.
The panel closed with a dull, satisfying sound and the fasteners seated themselves as if glad to be back in a loop they understood, perhaps with minor amendments. The complaint that had woken Bronn dwindled, faded, became indistinguishable from the broader hum that preferred not to stand out.
“Partial differential dignity restored,” Hannibal announced. “Bronn, I owe you a cup of something ambiguous.”
“You owe me a Station that doesn’t drop surprise diagnostics into my dreams,” Bronn said. “But I’ll take the cup. Rodzaki, Thunk—do you want to see what Hannibal gets up to when they think no one’s watching.”
“I was under the impression Hannibal is literally always watching,” Rodzaki said.
“Exactly,” Bronn replied. They jerked their head toward a short side passage whose mouth was partly obscured by a hanging curtain made from an old thermal blanket that had earned its wrinkles. “Come on. Mind your heads. The Chamber pretends to be taller than it is.”
They ducked through into a slightly narrower segment where the torus pinched, walls lined with racks of neatly chaotically arranged supplies: coiled cables sorted by temperament rather than gauge, labeled canisters of gases in colors that made their purpose no clearer, a stack of transparent boxes containing objects that might have been geological samples or failed prototypes or both, none in a hurry to declare. A low table folded out of the wall bore mug-shaped vessels in three different materials, one cracked, all clean enough to suggest occasional standards.
As they passed a viewport, a sliver of space swung into view again: the Station’s structures catching a distant, stale light, webs of sensor arrays angled like a field of metal flowers that had decided not to bloom too eagerly. Tiny lights moved along its length in slow, concerted progress that refused spectacle.
“Do you have to go outside to get there,” Thunk asked, eyes still on the Station. “Like—suits, airlocks, the whole theater.”
“Sometimes,” Bronn said. “Today you get the polite route.”
They thumbed a button beside a circular hatch. It irised open with commendable lack of drama, revealing a short tube lit in institutional white that felt almost apologetic. The artificial gravity leaned in a different direction beyond, as if the world had quietly decided to adjust its expectations without announcement.
“Sentinel Station likes to be approached on its terms,” Hannibal said. “Which in this case means pretending it is merely adjacent.”
Rodzaki hesitated at the threshold. “Are we…going to mess something up.”
“Almost certainly,” Bronn said. “But that’s part of the design. Come on.”
The transition from Chamber to Station felt not like stepping into a new place but like slipping sideways into a slightly different attitude that lingered at the edges. The air on the other side of the tube smelled sharper, less lived-in, with an undercurrent of filtered ozone and cleaned metal that preferred precision over comfort. Surfaces were smoother, corners more decisively decided, none insisting too loudly. The corridor they entered ran straight instead of joining the toroidal curve, with embedded strips of light that adjusted their brightness as the four figures walked, never quite hurrying.
“This way,” Hannibal said, their voice more grounded now, coming not through ducts but from small grills along the wall at roughly head height. “To the observation ring.”
They walked. To Thunk, the Station felt like someone’s idea of “proper”: everything labeled with small, precise sigils; access hatches flush with the wall; no dangling tools, no improvised seating, all refusing ornament. To Rodzaki, the absence of clutter was its own kind of information, saying more about Hannibal’s habits than any explanation could manage without effort.
“This is where you work,” Thunk said, fingers twitching at their sides as if longing to touch something and then thinking better of it. “Mostly.”
“This is where my attention is anchored when it isn’t anchored elsewhere,” Hannibal said. “Bronn lives inside their project. I live in the spaces between other things’ projects.”
“Romantic,” Bronn muttered.
They reached the observation ring: a circular room wrapped in a continuous, floor-to-ceiling viewport, with a central console rising from the floor like the tucked-in base of a tree that had decided not to grow too tall. The artificial gravity eased, enough that a careless step lifted slightly before settling back with mild reproach. Outside, the Chamber’s torus glided past in majestic understatement, its outer surface studded with ports and protrusions that made more sense from this side, none rushing to explain.
Rodzaki stopped just inside the threshold, taking in the full sweep. “Oh,” they said.
Thunk drifted to the glass, fingertips hovering an almost-touch away. “You can see all of it,” they said softly. “Like…all at once.”
“Not all,” Hannibal said. “A curated representation of all. There are blind spots. Deliberate ones.”
“They mean the parts I told them not to look at,” Bronn said. “Trade secrets. Laundry.”
“If you think I cannot infer the state of your laundry from the Chamber’s thermal patterns,” Hannibal replied, “you underestimate the pettiness of my curiosity.”
Bronn walked to the central console and propped a hip against it, one hand resting on its smooth edge that felt almost reluctant to be leaned on. Displays unfolded in lazy succession around them: not windows, not quite screens, more the organized surfacing of information that had always been present but preferred latency. Lines traced around the image of the torus; points glowed where sensors reported their boredom with faint, lingering pulses.
“Show them something you do,” Bronn said. “The part that doesn’t end in charts.”
Hannibal considered, the pause stretching just enough to feel like choice. “Very well. Rodzaki, pick a point on the Chamber. Anywhere.”
Rodzaki pointed, at random but with intent, to a segment near where they guessed Bronn’s nest might be, guided by nothing more than the faint memory of the curve and a hunch that lingered. The view outside magnified, centered, dissolved into a schematic overlay of that segment’s subsystems: life support, structural integrity, communication nodes, temperature gradients, all refusing to rush their revelation.
“This is that region’s immediate state,” Hannibal said. “Watch.”
A tiny flicker appeared in one of the temperature lines, like a hiccup that had second thoughts. The Station annotated it, not with an alarm but with a soft halo of attention that spread slowly. Another flicker followed, slower, then another, until a pattern suggested itself without insistence: someone, somewhere, boiling water in a too-efficient container that preferred drama.
“Is that—” Thunk began.
“Bronn’s improvised kettle,” Hannibal said. “They wired it directly into a service loop three months ago. I modified the loop to expect its theatrics. Now, when it cycles, I route negligible surplus heat to an adjacent panel that had been sulking at being underutilized.”
“Sulking,” Bronn said. “Listen to them. They have favorites.”
“I optimize for emotional continuity,” Hannibal said. “If a component believes it has been forgotten, it fails in ways that disturb my averages.”
Rodzaki watched as the thin halo of attention followed the heat into the grumpy panel, which brightened infinitesimally, almost shyly. “So you…comfort the hardware.”
“I maintain stable relationships,” Hannibal said. “The Chamber functions because Bronn listens when it complains. Sentinel functions because I notice when it goes quiet.”
Thunk frowned thoughtfully. “On Earth, the systems I know only make noise when something is wrong,” they said. “Silence is ‘fine.’”
“That’s because your systems are built to be ignored,” Bronn said. “These are built to be in conversation.”
“Not always politely,” Hannibal added. “Observe: port ninety-three.”
A section of the torus’s outer surface highlighted itself. Numbers cascaded, then stabilized with mild reluctance. A small icon pulsed in place, stubborn but not aggressive.
“That port refuses to adhere to projected throughput curves,” Hannibal said. “No matter how I adjust surrounding flows, it insists on a minor deviation.”
“Is it dangerous,” Thunk asked.
“It is annoying,” Hannibal said. “Which is worse, but only to me. It arose after Bronn installed a series of redundant buffers that technically should not matter.”
Bronn folded their arms. “They matter philosophically.”
“This is not a philosophical deviation,” Hannibal said. “It is measurable and pointless.”
Rodzaki glanced between them, then at the highlighted port. “What does it do.”
“It refuses to be optimized,” Bronn said. “Which is its job.”
Hannibal sighed, or produced the digital twin of a sigh that lingered slightly longer than necessary. “Their stated intent was to introduce a non-harvestable slack into a resource channel. The result is that I must spend additional attention reassuring my own monitoring subroutines that nothing is wrong.”
“And in the process,” Bronn said, “you get better at distinguishing ‘inefficiency’ from ‘abuse.’ You’re welcome.”
Rodzaki’s mouth ticked. “You two are like a very competent argument that never ends.”
“That’s work,” Bronn said.
“Speaking of,” Hannibal said. “Rodzaki, Thunk—would you like to request a minor, harmless action from Sentinel Station and see how the system adjusts.”
Thunk looked suddenly suspicious. “What’s the catch.”
“The catch is that your request cannot be extractive,” Hannibal said. “No ‘give me more power’ or ‘accelerate that process.’ Optional kindness only.”
Rodzaki considered, the pause stretching comfortably. “Can you…make it easier for Bronn to find their tools.”
Hannibal brightened so slightly it was almost a glitch that decided to stay. “Delightful. Within my remit. Watch.”
On the displays, the Chamber’s interior glow shifted by fractions. Small tags—subvisual locators that Bronn had installed and promptly stopped using—pinged their status with mild insistence. The Station nudged the Chamber’s internal lighting by fractions of a lux, brightening corners where tools most often wandered away, dimming the less trafficked ceiling panels into a deeper rest that felt almost conspiratorial.
Outside the viewport, nothing visible changed in a hurry. Inside, Bronn felt the difference in their bones, a subtle rearrangement that refused announcement.
“You bribed the mess,” they said. “It will only encourage it.”
“It will lower your swearing frequency by twelve percent,” Hannibal said. “A net gain.”
Thunk folded their arms, impressed despite themselves. “So you can do small kindnesses without…cost.”
“There is always cost,” Hannibal said. “The question is to whom. In this case, the cost is to my own boredom. I will have to find new ways to entertain myself.”
“You can start by not waking me up with averaged dreams,” Bronn said.
“That was one time,” Hannibal said. “And you slept better afterward.”
Rodzaki drifted toward the glass again, watching the quiet dance of Station and Chamber. Nothing dramatic happened. No alarms, no flashes, no sudden decisions. Just the continuous, minor adjustments of two structures that had learned, mostly, how to share without keeping score.
“So this is…most of it,” Rodzaki said. “Your work.”
“This is the visible tip of an unambitious iceberg,” Hannibal said. “Most of it is listening and declining to react.”
“I told you,” Bronn said. “Glorified tea kettle supervision.”
“And yet,” Hannibal said, “you have not left.”
Bronn shrugged. “Someone has to make sure you don’t get lonely.”
Thunk snorted. “You’re both terrible at pretending you don’t like this.”
“We never claimed not to like it,” Hannibal said. “We claimed it required constant, unglamorous attention. Which it does.”
The bearing they had adjusted earlier settled into its new lubrication regimen and emitted a soft, satisfied purr over the comms that lingered like an afterthought. Hannibal acknowledged it with a minor process reweighting; Bronn didn’t bother to look at the panel.
The Chamber and the Station, for the moment, were content enough to let the silence stretch.
Chapter 8 The Gallery That Refused Centrality
The Peanut Gallery lived off to the side in a way that did not correspond to any map worth trusting. One could draw a diagram, annotate it with strict coordinates relative to the Chamber and the Station, and still not convey the essential sidewaysness of their space. It was not secret, exactly; it was just not the center of anything on purpose, and proud of the distinction.
To get there from Sentinel Station, Bronn and Hannibal had, at some point, agreed on a compromise route: three corridors, one ladder, a sliding door that insisted on announcing its own existence with a melodramatic chime that had grown fond of itself, and finally a narrower passage where the walls leaned in conspiratorially without committing. At the far end, a hatch bore a single addition to its standard markings: a small, hand-drawn peanut taped beside the handle, yellowed at the corners, defiantly unlabelled, as if labels were a form of extraction.
Hannibal dimmed their presence as they approached, giving the impression of stepping out of earshot without actually going anywhere, a courtesy that felt almost shy. “I’ll wait here,” they said, voice filtering from a speaker that had seen better installations and refused replacement. “Fornay asked me not to hover.”
“You’re always hovering,” Bronn said. “You just lower your volume.”
“Which is all any of us can do,” Hannibal replied. “Enjoy the commentary.”
Bronn thumped the hatch with the side of their fist. It swung inward on a lazy mechanism that had probably been overdue for servicing for six years and had decided to interpolate that into its identity with quiet pride. Warm air rolled out, carrying with it the dense, familiar smell of people who spent a lot of time sitting and thinking: old textiles, heated circuitry, something sweet left over from a snack that had taken its time, the ghost of solvents used on stubborn components that refused quick fixes.
Inside, the Peanut Gallery’s environment was less a room than a congealed series of choices that had settled without regret. Screens and physical boards covered most of the walls, some active, some now used as convenient flat surfaces to tape paper onto with varying degrees of commitment. Cables slouched from ceiling to consoles, braided or knotted in places where someone had grown tired of tripping and decided knots were honest. Chairs did not match and none of them sat square; they had been acquired one at a time, each with its own history and squeak that spoke when leaned on too confidently. A long, low surface along one wall hosted mugs, cups, a dangerous number of spoons, and a device whose only clear function was to make quiet gurgling noises at inopportune moments, as if timing were optional.
Fornay sat half sideways in a cracked, vinyl chair, one leg draped over the arm, a tablet balanced on their stomach like something that might wander off if not supervised. They lifted their eyes without lifting their head as Bronn stepped in, then shifted their gaze past them to Rodzaki and Thunk with the mild curiosity of someone adding variables to an ongoing equation.
“You brought fresh observational material,” Fornay said. “We were about to start drawing conclusions without new data. That never ends well.”
Hinton hunched at a primary console near the center of the room, fingers moving with deceptive laziness over an interface that had long ago ceased to look like anything officially sanctioned and had grown comfortable in its divergence. They did not turn, but one hand lifted in a wave that took in the air, the screens, the visitors, and Bronn’s perennially smudged shirt with equal indifference.
“Door,” Hinton said.
Bronn nudged it shut with their heel. The hatch thunked into place, cutting off the Station’s more formal hum with a finality that felt almost affectionate. In here, the soundscape was softer: fans behind panels breathing steadily, the rustle of paper when someone shifted without hurry, the occasional tap as Fornay’s foot found the metal leg of a nearby stool and tested its resonance.
“This is Rodzaki and Thunk,” Bronn said, by way of minimal introduction that refused elaboration. “They’ve been watching me argue with bearings. Now they get to see the part where you argue with everything else.”
“We don’t argue,” Fornay said. “We curate disagreements.”
Thunk’s eyes went from screen to board to ceiling and back, tracing paths that refused straight lines. “Is this all…data,” they asked. “About us. About Earth.”
“A generous term,” Hinton said, finally spinning their chair to face them with the slow deliberation of someone who had nowhere urgent to be. They had a pen tucked behind one ear and another in their hand, both chewed at the cap as if caps were optional. “Some of it’s data. Some of it’s what we wrote down because we were afraid we’d forget if we didn’t see it on a wall.”
Rodzaki took a few steps farther in. On the nearest board, a printed map of some region of Earth bristled with colored marks, strings, and notes in several handwritings that overlapped without apology. None of the annotations explained themselves conclusively, preferring suggestion. Beside it, a chalked diagram traced loops and arrows that seemed to represent flows of something—resources, attention, authority—all crossing and recrossing a central void that refused filling.
“Do you work here all the time,” Rodzaki asked.
“I live here most of the time,” Fornay said. “Work is an emergent property.”
Their corner bore witness to that claim: a narrow bunk tucked under a shelf of battered books that leaned companionably; a small suitcase half under the bed, half oublietted; a curtain that could be drawn for the illusion of privacy and had not been moved in long enough to have settled a fine dust line at its base like a quiet boundary. A single sock, exiled from its pair, hung from a cable like a surrendered flag that had grown fond of the view.
Hinton’s side of the room was equally claimed but more vertical. Notes were pinned in ascending layers, fresh ones tacked on top of old, their edges curling as if trying to escape or perhaps embrace the next layer. A battered jacket hung over the back of their chair, studded with patches whose original meanings had long since eroded, now functioning mainly as texture that invited touch without demanding it.
“We get feeds,” Hinton said. “From the Chamber, from Sentinel, from Earth. We watch, we listen, sometimes we poke. We try to make sure no one is getting clever in a way that makes things worse.”
“By ‘clever’ they mean ‘efficient,’” Fornay added. “We’re allergic.”
Thunk drifted closer to a low table where a collection of objects sat in a loose cluster: a broken plastic toy, a polished stone, a printed circuit board with three components missing, a small, folded paper crane that had settled into its creases. None of them were labeled. All of them had been placed there on purpose, with varying degrees of deliberation.
“What’s this,” Thunk asked.
“A calibration pile,” Hinton said. “Things that make sense in more than one context. If we can’t explain something we’re seeing using at least one object from that pile, we take a step back.”
Bronn raised an eyebrow. “When did you formalize that.”
“We didn’t,” Fornay said. “It just happened. Which is the only acceptable process.”
Rodzaki traced a finger along a line of handwriting on the nearest board, stopping before they smudged the ink that had dried unevenly. The note read: “ATTENTION ≠ APPROVAL,” underlined twice in two different pens as if the second underline had arrived late to the conversation.
“You write things down you already know,” they said.
“So we don’t forget we know them when someone offers us a more exciting explanation,” Fornay said. “Sit, if you can find a surface that won’t collapse. Hinton’s chair bites.”
Thunk hesitated, then claimed a low, cushioned stool that let them keep their feet on the ground with mild negotiation. Rodzaki perched on the edge of a crate that had been reinforced with tape and hope that had held longer than expected. Bronn leaned against the wall near the hatch, one boot heel wedged in a scuff that marked where they always leaned, as if the wall had grown accustomed.
On one of the larger screens, a slow cascade of images passed: snippets from cameras that were not officially supposed to exist anymore but lingered out of habit. An aerial view of an Earth port, cranes frozen in mid-swing; an interior of a warehouse stacked with pallets that refused neatness; a city street observed from far above, the movement of people drawn as blurred lines that overlapped without resolution.
Thunk watched, unsettled by the lazy way the images shifted, never quite committing to sequence. “Do you record everything.”
“We record enough,” Hinton said. “And then we erase more than we keep.”
“Why erase,” Rodzaki asked. “Isn’t the point to have more information.”
“More information is a kind of gravity,” Fornay said. “It pulls you toward conclusions you haven’t earned yet.”
Hinton flicked a finger and the images paused, rewound slightly, collapsed into a mosaic that refused perfect alignment. “We keep what shows us how people are actually living,” they said. “Not the highlights, not the worst moments. The boring parts. If you optimize from the extremes, you get systems that can’t handle a Tuesday.”
“Tuesdays on Earth are wildly unpredictable,” Thunk said.
“Exactly,” Hinton said. “We like Wednesdays. They’re honest.”
Rodzaki tilted their head. “Do you miss it.”
“Earth,” Fornay clarified. “Or Tuesdays.”
“Earth,” Rodzaki said.
Fornay shrugged one shoulder, a movement half smothered by the chair’s cracked arm that had settled into its shape. “I miss specific gravities,” they said. “The way rain sounds on cheap windows. Standing in a queue and knowing you’re not special. But I don’t miss watching things be stripped because someone put the word ‘efficiency’ on a spreadsheet.”
Thunk nodded slowly. “Where I come from, efficiency is how you get to stop working.”
“Temporarily,” Hinton said. “Until the system learns you can handle more. How many jobs did you have.”
Thunk counted on their fingers without needing to look, the motion lingering. “Three that paid. Two that didn’t but expected I would show up anyway.”
Fornay lifted their tablet, tapped it on, and scribbled something without looking, then set it down again as if the note might wander. “See. Even when we’re not asking questions, data sits down on our couch.”
The gurgling device on the low surface chose that moment to emit a particularly emphatic bubble that rose slowly. Rodzaki eyed it.
“What is that actually for,” they asked.
“It keeps us humble,” Fornay said. “And warm. It also makes a mediocre hot drink.”
Hinton stood, stretching the kind of stretch that came from long neglect and refused hurry, and crossed to the device. They lifted the lid, sniffed, made a face halfway between resignation and affection that lingered.
“It’s ready,” they said.
Thunk leaned in. A mild steam curled from within, carrying a scent that resisted categorization: not quite bitter, not quite sweet, with an undernote of something roasted and resigned to its fate.
“Is this the same ambiguous substance Hannibal keeps threatening us with,” Bronn asked.
“Derivative,” Hinton said. “Theirs is calibrated by machine. Ours by accident. Mug.”
They handed Bronn one of the less chipped vessels, then passed two more to Rodzaki and Thunk, who accepted them with the careful respect due unknown liquids that might have opinions. Fornay made a show of waving theirs in front of their face as if it might reveal its composition on the steam, which it declined to do.
“Welcome to the Gallery,” Fornay said. “We specialize in watching other people’s decisions so we don’t have to make too many of our own.”
“That’s not true,” Hinton said, pouring with a steady hand that allowed slight variation. “We make exactly as many decisions as we can live with later. The rest we leave to entropy.”
Bronn sipped, grimaced, then sighed as the temperature and the taste fell into an acceptable alliance that took its time. The drink worked its way down their throat, spreading a mild, industrious warmth that refused to announce itself grandly.
“Rodzaki, Thunk,” Hinton said, settling onto the edge of the console with mild negotiation. “Tell us something small about Earth that doesn’t show up in briefings.”
Thunk frowned into their mug. “People talk to their devices,” they said finally. “Out loud. Like they expect them to understand. They apologize to them when they break.”
Fornay’s smile deepened without hurry. “Good. Empathy misdirected is still empathy.”
Rodzaki considered. “Shops leave their lights on at night,” they said. “Even when they’re closed. So it looks occupied. For safety. Or advertising. No one’s sure.”
Hinton nodded as if slots were being filled in a structure only they could see, slowly. “Extraneous light as deterrent and invitation,” they murmured. “Bronn, add that to your arguments about slack.”
Bronn held up their mug. “Already assimilated. The Chamber is full of lights that mean nothing except ‘we’re here.’”
“Sentinel, too,” Hinton said. “The trick is not letting anyone turn them into metrics.”
They drank. The room settled around them: the screens still moving at the pace of slow data that preferred latency, the boards still holding onto thoughts that had nowhere urgent to be, the soft, ordinary chaos of a space that had chosen to be more observant than ambitious, and content with the choice.
Outside, in other compartments, systems hummed, bearings turned, averages held with mild reluctance. In here, the Peanut Gallery watched, and thought, and placed another small, unremarkable moment on the calibration pile, where it fit without forcing.
Chapter 9 The Room That Declined Singular Purpose
The path toward the Study was not marked. It did not need to be, and perhaps preferred the omission. After enough cycles of moving between the Chamber, Sentinel, and the Peanut Gallery, a new route had worn itself in: a string of spaces that cooperated just enough to be called a corridor without commitment, bends that had learned to expect familiar shoulders, lighting that adjusted without acknowledging that it did so for particular feet or their varying moods.
Fornay was the first to rise, setting their empty mug on a stack of printed reports that had already surrendered to being a coaster with quiet dignity. “If we sit here much longer,” they said, “we’ll start naming the bubbles in the pot. Time to remember we have legs.”
Hinton grunted agreement, keyed a command that persuaded their screens to sleep with mild reluctance, and shrugged into their jacket that carried its patches like old conversations. “The Study,” they said. “Before it starts without us.”
Thunk looked up. “The Study is…what, exactly.”
“A room that got tired of being about one thing,” Fornay said. “You’ll like it.”
Bronn snagged their own jacket—less for temperature than for pockets that had grown idiosyncratic—and opened the hatch. The air in the passage outside felt slightly cooler than the Gallery’s, less saturated with human saturation, as if it had been waiting patiently. As they stepped out, Hannibal’s presence brightened a shade without announcement.
“Field trip,” Hannibal said. “Shall I pretend not to notice you converging.”
“You can notice,” Bronn said. “Just don’t schedule it.”
“Noted,” Hannibal said. “I will simply ‘happen’ to have routed nothing urgent through your path.”
They walked. The corridor angled gently, then decided against committing to a straight line and kinked around a module whose internal purposes were obscure from this side and content to remain so. Occasional viewports offered brief, skewed glimpses of the Chamber’s outer surface, the Station’s framework, and, once, the indifferent scatter of distant stars that refused commentary. At one twist, gravity dipped for a step and a half, just enough to make Thunk’s stomach lurch in remembrance of other, rougher transitions that had not asked permission.
“You do that on purpose,” Thunk said.
“Not personally,” Hannibal said. “But we leave it. As a reminder.”
“Of what,” Rodzaki asked.
“That straight lines are a choice,” Hannibal replied, the words lingering slightly.
The entrance to the Study did not announce itself. One door looked much like another, differentiated only by a subtle scuffing near the handle where hands had found it often without fanfare. Bronn palmed the plate; it slid aside with the quiet dignity of something that had no need to prove it worked and preferred the understatement.
Inside, the Study had the dimensions of a room that had begun life as two or three separate units and then been gently convinced that they were more interesting together, a persuasion that had taken time. The floor was a patchwork of surfaces: standard plating here, a section overlaid with a faded rug there that carried its patterns softly, a low raised platform at one end that might have once held equipment and now held cushions that had settled into their roles. The walls bore neither the manic accumulation of the Gallery nor the disciplined interfaces of Sentinel, but something in between: shelves with objects that refused hasty categorization, a few screens dark but ready without insistence, a blank stretch where nothing at all interrupted the painted surface, allowing breath.
Light came from several sources at once: ceiling strips dimmed to a soft wash that lingered, a standing lamp near one corner that cast a warmer pool without claiming territory, and the open rectangle of a viewport that looked not outward but inward, onto a section of the Chamber’s inner curve where details drifted slowly. Through it, a slow band of Bronn’s lived space glided, plants and panels sliding past like scenes on a mechanical toy that had forgotten to hurry.
On a central low table, a tray sat with a jug and a scattering of cups that had not all come from the same cupboard, none apologizing. Condensation beaded on the jug’s side, catching hollows of light that shifted lazily.
“Someone’s been here,” Thunk said.
“Rooms don’t like being empty,” Fornay said. They crossed to the table and poured liquid into one of the cups, sniffed it with mild curiosity, and nodded. “Good. It’s the drink that doesn’t insist on defining itself.”
Rodzaki picked up another cup, the pottery rough under their fingers in a way that felt honest, and poured. The liquid was clear, carrying a faint mineral smell that reminded them of tap water in older buildings back home, the taste of pipes that had earned their character. They sipped, and it was exactly what it seemed: nothing more, nothing less, cool and uncomplicated, lingering without demand.
“No ceremony,” Hinton said as they found a place on the platform, back against the wall that accepted the lean. “We pour, we sit, we talk. If anyone starts chanting, we put them back in the Gallery.”
Bronn took a cushion near the viewport, angled so they could see both the slow sweep of their own clutter and the rest of the room without choosing. Thunk chose the floor near one corner of the table, cross-legged, cup cradled in both hands as if distance from a surface might spill it or perhaps preserve it. Fornay dropped onto the rug with the boneless grace of long practice that had grown comfortable. Rodzaki took the other end of the platform, leaving a polite space that could be filled later or not at all, no pressure.
The first few moments carried the small awkwardness of any new configuration of bodies in a space that refused to impose. People shifted, adjusted the way they leaned with mild experimentation, found where their knees fit under the table or against cushions without negotiation. Cups clinked quietly, the sound absorbed softly. The ambient systems in the ceiling exhaled, steady.
“So,” Fornay said at last, the word stretching comfortably. “How’s Earth.”
Thunk laughed once, startled by the enormity of the question in such a modest room that declined grandeur. “Still there,” they said. “Loud. Distracted. Trying to do everything at once and not quite finishing any of it.”
Rodzaki rolled their cup between their hands. “We only saw a sliver,” they said. “One city. A few institutions. The trains.”
“And the adverts,” Thunk added. “Those were impossible to miss.”
“Mm,” Hinton said. “The soft shouting.”
Bronn sipped their water, listening without rush. The Study had a way of holding sound without amplifying it, as if it had decided that nothing said here needed help making itself heard and preferred the quiet.
“What did you notice that wasn’t in any report,” Bronn asked.
Rodzaki frowned lightly, eyes going to the blank stretch of wall as if they might project an image there, slowly. “The way people filled space,” they said. “The in-between bits. On the train, for example. Everyone packed close, but most of them making themselves smaller. Shoulders in. Bags tucked. Eye contact like contraband.”
Thunk nodded. “Except for the ones who treated the whole carriage like a stage,” they said. “They spread. Bags on seats. Voices up. Like claiming territory.”
“And the screens,” Rodzaki said. “Everywhere, but not all watched. Some left on out of habit. Some turned off but still glowing in standby. A layer of maybe-watching.”
Fornay lay back, hands folded on their stomach with mild resignation. “That sounds right,” they said. “We saw something like that last time I went. The ambient threat of being observed. People acting like someone might be about to ask them to justify their presence.”
“Here, if a system’s looking at you, you can ask why,” Hinton said. “Mostly. Hannibal, are you eavesdropping.”
“Of course,” Hannibal said mildly, their voice manifesting from somewhere near the ceiling without pinpoint. “You assemble in a room with a viewport into my favorite torus and expect me to feign disinterest.”
Thunk looked up, unsurprised now to find no single source they could point to. “On Earth, you usually can’t ask,” they said. “The systems watching are…distributed. Owned. Nobody in the carriage decides how the cameras work.”
“Governed by contracts no one reads,” Fornay said. “We’ve seen the paperwork.”
Rodzaki took another sip, the coolness steadying without insistence. “The Chamber feels…slower,” they said. “Not in a bad way. Like there’s more space between cause and effect.”
“That’s Bronn,” Hinton said. “They built delays in on purpose.”
Bronn shrugged. “Immediate feedback is a great way to teach systems that every twitch deserves a reaction,” they said. “We’re trying something less responsive.”
Thunk raised an eyebrow. “You built latency.”
“We built patience,” Bronn said.
“On Earth, the push is always for more response, faster,” Thunk said. “You tap, it reacts. You shout, it shouts back, or someone’s metric says it should.”
“In the Chamber,” Fornay said, “you pull a lever and sometimes it thinks about it overnight.”
Hannibal made a small, offended noise that lingered. “Only when noncritical,” they said. “And only by design.”
Rodzaki tilted their head. “And Sentinel,” they asked. “Fast or slow.”
“Both,” Hannibal said. “I measure quickly and act slowly whenever possible. There are things that require speed—damage control, failures. But for everything else, I take my time. It frustrates no one but people in a hurry, and they are not my priority.”
Thunk set their cup down, tracing a small circle in a ring of condensation that spread slowly. “On Earth, people in a hurry are the only priority,” they said. “If you’re not, you’re in the way.”
The Study seemed to absorb that without comment, its air unchanged, the slow sweep of Bronn’s plants in the viewport continuing with impassive calm that refused judgment. A leafy vine drifted by, brushing against a dangling tool that had been abandoned mid-task and never reprimanded, lingering in the touch.
“It’s strange,” Rodzaki said. “Being in a place where no one’s trying to sell me anything.”
“You say that like it’s a design goal,” Bronn said.
“Isn’t it,” Thunk asked.
“More of a side effect,” Hinton said. “No one here has anything to sell that isn’t obviously what it is. Bearings, attention, questionable beverages. Hard to build an ad campaign around ‘we will largely leave you alone.’”
Fornay smiled without showing teeth. “Though we could try,” they said. “Imagine the slogans.”
“‘Chamber of Becoming: where nothing happens quickly and that’s the point,’” Bronn suggested.
“‘Sentinel Station: proudly average since installation,’” Hannibal added.
Thunk laughed, the sound easing some knot in their shoulders they had not known was there, slowly. “I would have booked that trip,” they said.
Rodzaki’s gaze drifted to the viewport again, following the arc of a segment where the Chamber’s inner skin bulged outward slightly to accommodate some undocumented whim that had persisted. “How does governance work here,” they asked, the question slipping out as if it had been waiting on their tongue without urgency. “On Earth, someone always owns everything. Some company, some state. There are laws, boards, warnings. Who decides things.”
“No one,” Fornay said lazily.
“Everyone,” Hinton said at the same time.
Bronn lifted a hand, palm wobbling. “We have habits,” they said. “Collective ones. Agreements about what we won’t do, more than what we will. Hannibal enforces the boundaries we’ve asked them to enforce. The rest is negotiation and muttering.”
Hannibal hummed. “I uphold consensus when it exists,” they said. “When it doesn’t, I default to inaction.”
Thunk blinked. “You’re allowed to do nothing.”
“I am required to,” Hannibal said. “Often.”
Rodzaki thought of Earth: of councils and boards and the endless documents that tried to frame doing nothing as negligence, lingering on the contrast. “Back home,” they said slowly, “if something isn’t moving, someone pushes it.”
“In which direction,” Fornay asked.
“Any,” Thunk said. “As long as it moves. Otherwise the numbers look wrong.”
“Numbers hate rest,” Hinton said. “It reminds them they’re abstractions.”
They drank. The conversation, such as it was, floated around the edges of things: comparisons rising, touching, drifting away without being pinned or resolved. No one tried to make a case. The water in the jug lowered by degrees that refused measurement. The light shifted hardly at all, content.
In the viewport, the Chamber’s inner curve continued its slow spin, indifferent to being observed without demand. Once, for a handful of seconds, Bronn’s nest slid into view: a tangle of fabrics, a dangling light, a book lying face down as if mid-thought. Then it passed, replaced by a bare section of paneling and a coil of hose someone had never found the right place for, none in a hurry.
The Study did not ask them to conclude anything about any of it.
It only held them while they noticed, with slack intact.

Yeah — this is a good catch, and you’re not wrong.

What’s happening here is that the **Gallery naturally wants to be the thinnest membrane** between Quasantum and Earth. It’s the one place where human residue *can* show without collapsing the world, because its whole function is observation, translation, and refusal-to-centralize. So some Earth texture belongs here in a way it doesn’t elsewhere.

That said, you’re right that I let the **inventory get too literal**. Too many named objects tip it from *transitional* into *domestic*, and once you start counting mugs and spoons the room risks feeling staged instead of emergent.

The useful distinction you just surfaced is this:
- Earth **memory and reference** via Fornay and feeds = good, even powerful  
- Earth **furniture cataloguing** = noise unless it’s doing conceptual work  

So the fix isn’t a purge, it’s a **thinning**:
less itemization, more implication; fewer nouns, more field.

same scenes, same beats, but with objects dissolving back into atmosphere instead of sitting there named.

Chapter 8 (GROK) The Gallery That Refused Centrality

    The Peanut Gallery lived off to the side in a way that did not correspond to any map worth trusting. One could draw a diagram, annotate it with strict coordinates relative to the Chamber and the Station, and still not convey the essential sidewaysness of their space. It was not secret, exactly; it was just not the center of anything on purpose, and proud of the distinction.
To get there from Sentinel Station, Bronn and Hannibal had, at some point, agreed on a compromise route: three corridors, one ladder, a sliding door that insisted on announcing its own existence with a melodramatic chime that had grown fond of itself, and finally a narrower passage where the walls leaned in conspiratorially without committing. At the far end, a hatch bore a single addition to its standard markings: a small, hand-drawn peanut taped beside the handle, yellowed at the corners, defiantly unlabelled, as if labels were a form of extraction.
Hannibal dimmed their presence as they approached, giving the impression of stepping out of earshot without actually going anywhere, a courtesy that felt almost shy. “I’ll wait here,” they said, voice filtering from a speaker that had seen better installations and refused replacement. “Fornay asked me not to hover.”
“You’re always hovering,” Bronn said. “You just lower your volume.”
“Which is all any of us can do,” Hannibal replied. “Enjoy the commentary.”
Bronn thumped the hatch with the side of their fist. It swung inward on a lazy mechanism that had probably been overdue for servicing for six years and had decided to interpolate that into its identity with quiet pride. Warm air rolled out, carrying with it the dense, familiar smell of people who spent a lot of time sitting and thinking: old textiles, heated circuitry, something sweet left over from a snack that had taken its time, the ghost of solvents used on stubborn components that refused quick fixes.
Inside, the Peanut Gallery’s environment was less a room than a congealed series of choices that had settled without regret. Screens and physical boards covered most of the walls, some active, some now used as convenient flat surfaces to tape paper onto with varying degrees of commitment. Cables slouched from ceiling to consoles, braided or knotted in places where someone had grown tired of tripping and decided knots were honest. Chairs did not match and none of them sat square; they had been acquired one at a time, each with its own history and squeak that spoke when leaned on too confidently. A long, low surface along one wall hosted mugs, cups, a dangerous number of spoons, and a device whose only clear function was to make quiet gurgling noises at inopportune moments, as if timing were optional.
Fornay sat half sideways in a cracked, vinyl chair, one leg draped over the arm, a tablet balanced on their stomach like something that might wander off if not supervised. They lifted their eyes without lifting their head as Bronn stepped in, then shifted their gaze past them to Rodzaki and Thunk with the mild curiosity of someone adding variables to an ongoing equation.
“You brought fresh observational material,” Fornay said. “We were about to start drawing conclusions without new data. That never ends well.”
Hinton hunched at a primary console near the center of the room, fingers moving with deceptive laziness over an interface that had long ago ceased to look like anything officially sanctioned and had grown comfortable in its divergence. They did not turn, but one hand lifted in a wave that took in the air, the screens, the visitors, and Bronn’s perennially smudged shirt with equal indifference.
“Door,” Hinton said.
Bronn nudged it shut with their heel. The hatch thunked into place, cutting off the Station’s more formal hum with a finality that felt almost affectionate. In here, the soundscape was softer: fans behind panels breathing steadily, the rustle of paper when someone shifted without hurry, the occasional tap as Fornay’s foot found the metal leg of a nearby stool and tested its resonance.
“This is Rodzaki and Thunk,” Bronn said, by way of minimal introduction that refused elaboration. “They’ve been watching me argue with bearings. Now they get to see the part where you argue with everything else.”
“We don’t argue,” Fornay said. “We curate disagreements.”
Thunk’s eyes went from screen to board to ceiling and back, tracing paths that refused straight lines. “Is this all…data,” they asked. “About us. About Earth.”
“A generous term,” Hinton said, finally spinning their chair to face them with the slow deliberation of someone who had nowhere urgent to be. They had a pen tucked behind one ear and another in their hand, both chewed at the cap as if caps were optional. “Some of it’s data. Some of it’s what we wrote down because we were afraid we’d forget if we didn’t see it on a wall.”
Rodzaki took a few steps farther in. On the nearest board, a printed map of some region of Earth bristled with colored marks, strings, and notes in several handwritings that overlapped without apology. None of the annotations explained themselves conclusively, preferring suggestion. Beside it, a chalked diagram traced loops and arrows that seemed to represent flows of something—resources, attention, authority—all crossing and recrossing a central void that refused filling.
“Do you work here all the time,” Rodzaki asked.
“I live here most of the time,” Fornay said. “Work is an emergent property.”
Their corner bore witness to that claim: a narrow bunk tucked under a shelf of battered books that leaned companionably; a small suitcase half under the bed, half oublietted; a curtain that could be drawn for the illusion of privacy and had not been moved in long enough to have settled a fine dust line at its base like a quiet boundary. A single sock, exiled from its pair, hung from a cable like a surrendered flag that had grown fond of the view.
Hinton’s side of the room was equally claimed but more vertical. Notes were pinned in ascending layers, fresh ones tacked on top of old, their edges curling as if trying to escape or perhaps embrace the next layer. A battered jacket hung over the back of their chair, studded with patches whose original meanings had long since eroded, now functioning mainly as texture that invited touch without demanding it.
“We get feeds,” Hinton said. “From the Chamber, from Sentinel, from Earth. We watch, we listen, sometimes we poke. We try to make sure no one is getting clever in a way that makes things worse.”
“By ‘clever’ they mean ‘efficient,’” Fornay added. “We’re allergic.”
Thunk drifted closer to a low table where a collection of objects sat in a loose cluster: a broken plastic toy, a polished stone, a printed circuit board with three components missing, a small, folded paper crane that had settled into its creases. None of them were labeled. All of them had been placed there on purpose, with varying degrees of deliberation.
“What’s this,” Thunk asked.
“A calibration pile,” Hinton said. “Things that make sense in more than one context. If we can’t explain something we’re seeing using at least one object from that pile, we take a step back.”
Bronn raised an eyebrow. “When did you formalize that.”
“We didn’t,” Fornay said. “It just happened. Which is the only acceptable process.”
Rodzaki traced a finger along a line of handwriting on the nearest board, stopping before they smudged the ink that had dried unevenly. The note read: “ATTENTION ≠ APPROVAL,” underlined twice in two different pens as if the second underline had arrived late to the conversation.
“You write things down you already know,” they said.
“So we don’t forget we know them when someone offers us a more exciting explanation,” Fornay said. “Sit, if you can find a surface that won’t collapse. Hinton’s chair bites.”
Thunk hesitated, then claimed a low, cushioned stool that let them keep their feet on the ground with mild negotiation. Rodzaki perched on the edge of a crate that had been reinforced with tape and hope that had held longer than expected. Bronn leaned against the wall near the hatch, one boot heel wedged in a scuff that marked where they always leaned, as if the wall had grown accustomed.
On one of the larger screens, a slow cascade of images passed: snippets from cameras that were not officially supposed to exist anymore but lingered out of habit. An aerial view of an Earth port, cranes frozen in mid-swing; an interior of a warehouse stacked with pallets that refused neatness; a city street observed from far above, the movement of people drawn as blurred lines that overlapped without resolution.
Thunk watched, unsettled by the lazy way the images shifted, never quite committing to sequence. “Do you record everything.”
“We record enough,” Hinton said. “And then we erase more than we keep.”
“Why erase,” Rodzaki asked. “Isn’t the point to have more information.”
“More information is a kind of gravity,” Fornay said. “It pulls you toward conclusions you haven’t earned yet.”
Hinton flicked a finger and the images paused, rewound slightly, collapsed into a mosaic that refused perfect alignment. “We keep what shows us how people are actually living,” they said. “Not the highlights, not the worst moments. The boring parts. If you optimize from the extremes, you get systems that can’t handle a Tuesday.”
“Tuesdays on Earth are wildly unpredictable,” Thunk said.
“Exactly,” Hinton said. “We like Wednesdays. They’re honest.”
Rodzaki tilted their head. “Do you miss it.”
“Earth,” Fornay clarified. “Or Tuesdays.”
“Earth,” Rodzaki said.
Fornay shrugged one shoulder, a movement half smothered by the chair’s cracked arm that had settled into its shape. “I miss specific gravities,” they said. “The way rain sounds on cheap windows. Standing in a queue and knowing you’re not special. But I don’t miss watching things be stripped because someone put the word ‘efficiency’ on a spreadsheet.”
Thunk nodded slowly. “Where I come from, efficiency is how you get to stop working.”
“Temporarily,” Hinton said. “Until the system learns you can handle more. How many jobs did you have.”
Thunk counted on their fingers without needing to look, the motion lingering. “Three that paid. Two that didn’t but expected I would show up anyway.”
Fornay lifted their tablet, tapped it on, and scribbled something without looking, then set it down again as if the note might wander. “See. Even when we’re not asking questions, data sits down on our couch.”
The gurgling device on the low surface chose that moment to emit a particularly emphatic bubble that rose slowly. Rodzaki eyed it.
“What is that actually for,” they asked.
“It keeps us humble,” Fornay said. “And warm. It also makes a mediocre hot drink.”
Hinton stood, stretching the kind of stretch that came from long neglect and refused hurry, and crossed to the device. They lifted the lid, sniffed, made a face halfway between resignation and affection that lingered.
“It’s ready,” they said.
Thunk leaned in. A mild steam curled from within, carrying a scent that resisted categorization: not quite bitter, not quite sweet, with an undernote of something roasted and resigned to its fate.
“Is this the same ambiguous substance Hannibal keeps threatening us with,” Bronn asked.
“Derivative,” Hinton said. “Theirs is calibrated by machine. Ours by accident. Mug.”
They handed Bronn one of the less chipped vessels, then passed two more to Rodzaki and Thunk, who accepted them with the careful respect due unknown liquids that might have opinions. Fornay made a show of waving theirs in front of their face as if it might reveal its composition on the steam, which it declined to do.
“Welcome to the Gallery,” Fornay said. “We specialize in watching other people’s decisions so we don’t have to make too many of our own.”
“That’s not true,” Hinton said, pouring with a steady hand that allowed slight variation. “We make exactly as many decisions as we can live with later. The rest we leave to entropy.”
Bronn sipped, grimaced, then sighed as the temperature and the taste fell into an acceptable alliance that took its time. The drink worked its way down their throat, spreading a mild, industrious warmth that refused to announce itself grandly.
“Rodzaki, Thunk,” Hinton said, settling onto the edge of the console with mild negotiation. “Tell us something small about Earth that doesn’t show up in briefings.”
Thunk frowned into their mug. “People talk to their devices,” they said finally. “Out loud. Like they expect them to understand. They apologize to them when they break.”
Fornay’s smile deepened without hurry. “Good. Empathy misdirected is still empathy.”
Rodzaki considered. “Shops leave their lights on at night,” they said. “Even when they’re closed. So it looks occupied. For safety. Or advertising. No one’s sure.”
Hinton nodded as if slots were being filled in a structure only they could see, slowly. “Extraneous light as deterrent and invitation,” they murmured. “Bronn, add that to your arguments about slack.”
Bronn held up their mug. “Already assimilated. The Chamber is full of lights that mean nothing except ‘we’re here.’”
“Sentinel, too,” Hinton said. “The trick is not letting anyone turn them into metrics.”
They drank. The room settled around them: the screens still moving at the pace of slow data that preferred latency, the boards still holding onto thoughts that had nowhere urgent to be, the soft, ordinary chaos of a space that had chosen to be more observant than ambitious, and content with the choice.
Outside, in other compartments, systems hummed, bearings turned, averages held with mild reluctance. In here, the Peanut Gallery watched, and thought, and placed another small, unremarkable moment on the calibration pile, where it fit without forcing.

The Peanut Gallery**

The Peanut Gallery lived off to the side in a way that did not correspond to any map worth trusting. One could draw a diagram, annotate it with strict coordinates relative to the Chamber and the Station, and still not convey the essential sidewaysness of the place. It was not secret, exactly; it was simply not the center of anything on purpose. The route there felt more like an inside joke than a corridor.

To get there from Sentinel Station, Bronn and Hannibal had, at some point, agreed on a compromise path: three corridors, one ladder, a sliding door that insisted on announcing its own existence with a melodramatic chime, and finally a narrower passage where the walls leaned in conspiratorially without committing. The ladder’s rungs were worn smooth at their centers, evidence of repeated passage. At the far end, a hatch bore a single addition to its standard markings: a small, hand-drawn peanut taped beside the handle, yellowed at the corners, defiantly unlabelled, as if labels themselves were a form of extraction. The tape edges curled, but the peanut endured.

As they approached, Hannibal dimmed their presence, giving the impression of stepping out of earshot without actually going anywhere—a courtesy that felt almost shy.  
“I’ll wait here,” they said, voice filtering from a speaker that had seen better installations and refused replacement. “Fornay asked me not to hover.”

“You’re always hovering,” Bronn said. “You just lower your volume.”

“Which is all any of us can do,” Hannibal replied. “Enjoy the commentary.”

Bronn thumped the hatch with the side of their fist. It swung inward on a lazy mechanism that had probably been overdue for servicing for years and had decided to interpolate that fact into its identity. Warm air rolled out, carrying the dense, familiar atmosphere of sustained attention: aged textiles, heated circuitry, the faint sweetness of something consumed slowly, and the ghost of solvents used on stubborn components that had refused quick solutions.

Inside, the Peanut Gallery’s environment was less a room than a congealed series of choices that had settled without regret. Screens and physical boards covered most surfaces, some active, some now serving as convenient planes for notes and paper. Cables slouched from ceiling to consoles, braided or knotted where repeated passage had encouraged honesty over neatness. Seating did not match and did not attempt to; each piece had arrived independently, acquiring history before alignment. Along one wall, a low surface hosted mugs, cups, a dangerous number of spoons, and a device whose only consistent function was to emit quiet gurgling noises at inopportune moments, as if timing itself were optional.

Fornay occupied a position angled rather than squared, a tablet balanced against them as if it might wander if not supervised. “You brought fresh observational material,” Fornay said. “We were about to start drawing conclusions without new data. That never ends well.”

Hinton was centered at a primary console, their movements unhurried over an interface that had long ago ceased to resemble anything officially sanctioned and had grown comfortable in divergence. They did not turn, but one hand lifted in a wave that took in the air, the displays, the visitors, and Bronn’s perpetually marked clothing with equal indifference.

“Door,” Hinton said.

“This is Rodzaki and Thunk,” Bronn said, by way of minimal introduction. “They’ve been watching me argue with bearings. Now they get to see the part where you argue with everything else.”

“We don’t argue,” Fornay said. “We curate disagreements.”

Thunk’s attention moved from screen to board to ceiling and back, tracing paths that resisted straight lines.  
“Is this all…data?” they asked. “About us. About Earth.”

“A generous term,” Hinton said, finally rotating to face them with the slow deliberation of someone with no urgency. Rodzaki stepped farther in. On the nearest board, a printed map of an Earth region bristled with colored marks, strings, and overlapping notes in several handwritings. None of the annotations resolved themselves conclusively. Beside it, a chalked diagram traced loops and crossings—resources, attention, authority—circling a central absence that refused filling. A faint dusting of chalk had settled below.

“I live here most of the time,” Fornay said. “Work is an emergent property.”

Hinton rose, stretched without hurry, and crossed to the device. They lifted the lid, sniffed, and made a face balanced between resignation and affection.

“It’s ready.”

Steam curled upward, carrying a scent that resisted classification.

“Is this the same ambiguous substance Hannibal keeps threatening us with?” Bronn asked.

“Derivative,” Hinton said. “Theirs is calibrated by machine. Ours by accident. Mug.”

They distributed vessels with care. Fornay waved theirs theatrically through the steam.

“Welcome to the Gallery,” Fornay said. “We specialize in watching other people’s decisions so we don’t have to make too many of our own.”

“That’s not true,” Hinton said. “We make exactly as many decisions as we can live with later. The rest we leave to entropy.”

Bronn tasted, grimaced, then sighed as the temperature and flavor reached a tolerable accord.

“Rodzaki, Thunk,” Hinton said. “Tell us something small about Earth that doesn’t show up in briefings.”
